content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
t 102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 9581 t 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Eric Lucan 
Novato City Councilmember 
26 Truman Drive 
Novato, CA 94947 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-077 
Dear Mr. Lucan: 
April 24, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which 
may have already taken place. The Commission will decline to provide advice related to conduct 
that has already occurred. (Regulation 18329.) If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these 
decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May you participate in policy related discussions on establishing a process for "parklettes" 
on Grant Avenue in Downtown Novato? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Based on the small size of the "parklettes/' low intensity of the proposed use, and the 
buffer between your property and Grant A venue, you will not have a conflict of interest in the 
decisions. 
1 The Political Refc;mn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-077 
Page No. 2 
Earlier this year, a downtown merchant on Grant A venue applied for and was granted an 
encroachment permit with the City of Novato for a pilot "parklette" that would tum three public 
parking spaces into a temporary patio for outdoor dining for two years. That decision and 
subsequent construction of the outdoor patio has brought up questions about the future process for 
establishing similar "parklettes." At a future date, the City Council will probably be asked to weigh 
in and/or establish a process. Such future discussions might also include decisions about the 
"parklette" that was already constructed. 
' 
You have a real property interest in Downtown Novato at 1014 Machin A venue, consisting 
of a rental duplex with two I-bedroom units. Both units are rented. Grant Avenue is the main street 
in Downtown Novato. While Machin intersects Grant Avenue, your rental property is a block away 
with several commercial buildings lying between your rental property and Grant A venue. 
You do not believe the discussions, policy, or "parklette" will have any impact on your 
property with regards to traffic levels or intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise, air quality, 
etc., because your property has ample parking. In a telephone conversation on April 20, 2018, you 
noted that each unit has a garage and a driveway for parking. Also, the parking on Grant Avenue is 
time restricted, and thus not likely utilized by your tenants or their guests. You note that your 
property is as close as 185 feet to Grant Avenue at one location. And you also note that your 
property is as far away as a half of a mile for other portions of Grant A venue. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Under Section 87103(b), an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on any interest specified in Section 87103. 
With respect to the decisions at issue, you have a financial interest in residential real 
property under the Act. You also appear to have interests in your rental business as a business entity 
and source of income, as well as your tenants ~s a sourc~ of income. (See Section 87103(a) and (c).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision, and an interest is explicitly involved in the decision if (among other 
specific situations not applicable here) the interest is a named party, or subject of, the decision. 
(Regulation 18701 (a).) Your property is not "explicitly involved" in the decisions relating to the 
Project. Therefore, the effect of the decisions on their respective residences is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation 1870 I (b ). } 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's financial 
effect on an official's interest in a business entity, including real property containing a business 


File No. A-18-077 
Page No. 3 
entity, that is not explicitly involved in the decision. (See Regulation I 8702.2(a)(8).) Under 
Regulation 18702.l(b), a financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information 
would find it reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the 
business entity. Regulation 18702.2(a) sets forth the standards for determining whether the 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's financial 
interest in real property is material. For real property not explicitly involved in a decision, a 
foreseeable effect is material if the decision: 
( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property ... 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice 
allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if the Commission 
determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property; 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its· reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property. 
You indicate that permits for "parklettes" would be limited to Grant Avenue. Your property 
is not on Grant Avenue. In fact, it is located185 feet away, with several commercial buildings lying 
in between it and your property. Moreover, the "parklettes" themselves would be very small, 
consisting of three public parking spaces, which would serve as temporary patio for outdoor dining 
for two years. Your property also includes its own dedicated parking spaces. Thus, it is unlikely that 
any future "parklettes" would affect the character of your property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding your real property parcel, the 
view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property. Consequently, we conclude based on the small size of the 
"parklettes," low intensity of the proposed use, and the buffer between your property and Grant 
A venue, as well as the fact that the rental units have dedicated parking spaces, that there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable material effect on your rental business. Similarly, there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on your rental property. 
Barring additional facts, you do not have a conflict of interest in the decisions.2 
2 In regard to the tenants of your rental business, there is no foreseeable material effect on your economic 
interest in the tenants resulting from the lease as we have determined that there is no foreseeable material effect on the 
leased property or your rental business. However, we caution that your interest in a tenant may result in a disqualifying 
conflict of interest if you know or have reason to know that the tenant has any other interests in business entities or 
property implicated by the decision. (See Regulation 18702.3.) 
· 


File No. A-18-077 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
a~~~ 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
